replies.author: @thegardenofeatin5965
replies.text:
@AaronStrawn And it's layered.

My father makes a HUGE deal out of the older lady who "speaks Jive" in Airplane is the actress that played Mrs. Cleaver in Leave It To Beaver.  Now, my father has never watched a movie in his life because every time he's tried he spent the whole runtime talking over it saying "Oh do you know who that is?  You know that scene in Crocodile Dundee 2 where they go to the punk bar, and he throws his knife into the guy's mohawk?  That's the girl who was standing next to the mohawk guy!" But still, to him, it's funny that the actress that played that That character played *That* character.

Those of us who first saw the movie on VHS once we were old enough for it have never seen a single episode of Leave It To Beaver and saw a middle class white woman talk like young black men.  The joke works without the actor allusion.

The same thing works overall, because at the time Airplane! was in theaters, most of the cast was known as serious actors, so seeing them in this silly context would have been a novel experience.  But also just delivering this silly dialog as serious as triage is funny even without that context.  The world we're seeing into is absurd, the people in it take it perfectly seriously.

There is a moment in Deadpool 2 where...how does it work, Deadpool puts together X-Force, and they all get killed because of the wind, one of which is an invisible man, who we find out is Brad Pitt as he's being electrocuted.  Brad Pitt is there to show his face, he doesn't do any actual performing, on or against type.  In 40 years when our cumsprouts' cumsprouts rediscover that movie, that joke is going straight over their heads in a way the casting of Airplane! won't.

replies.author: @simval84
replies.text:
​@patmccrotch5373As a fan of the original movies, the new movie is a mixed bag but still worth seeing. Some funny jokes, Liam Neeson is very good, but there are also long periods where the jokes fall flat, especially the ending.

They also seem not to trust the audience enough to let the jokes be in the background too much. What would have happened in the periphery of the action in the old movies tends to be zoomed in in this one.

Anyway, as to the humor, it's a mix. They try some of the old Naked Gun humor, but also introduce some MacFarlane-style humor. I'm not saying the MacFarlane humor is bad, the body camera scene got some of the biggest laughs and it's classic Family Guy humor. But sometimes they tried toned down MacFarlane humor to fit the Naked Gun mood and that is when it fell flat.

I find the scene of the first trailer to be one of the low points of the movie, and it makes me wonder why they put so much focus on it. Having a girl scout pull of a mask to reveal Liam Neeson who then kicks ass just isn't funny, it's more cringe.

replies.author: @Wustenfuchs109
replies.text:
They can. If you read/listen about anything in that industry for more than a tik-tok video, you'll see that the studios have hundreds of fresh scripts around. They are constantly being sent brand new IPs and one shot stories. Why almost no one is doing them? Because you live in capitalism baby! The films in modern times cost an arm and a leg, so for a studio to film something, they need a guarantee it will be a good return on the investment.

It is not a bunch of people gathering with a camera - before all of that, there are huge meetings with investors, bankers, risk coefficients being calculated and re-calculated, how a certain investment can be salvaged even on a cinema flop and so on.

In a climate like that, you don't risk money on a brand new thing - you go and spend it on something you KNOW will make money. Even if it is a flop, if it keeps the franchise alive, you are going to do it. For example, Disney was willing to suck up all those SW losses because it kept the franchise relevant, (plus, grifting sells) and newer audience came... and started buying older things from the franchise. So new stuff could afford to be flops.

You simply have none of those things with brand new untested stuff. It could be a banger, a huge win, or it could be a major flop with absolutely no way of getting the money back in any shape or form. And if it was some small investment, sure, a studio could take a risk. But not at these prices.

Same with games - to make a game today costs MUCH more than 20 years ago. At least if you want one to compete on the AAA market. Which is why games today don't really innovate and are actually just recycling some old stuff with adding various monetization strategies.

That's what hypercapitalism always delivers. It must cater to the lowest common thing, and in the end it creates slop for no one. Because, that lowest common thing usually does not exist. It's a famous example in statistics - what an average man/woman is. You list all the qualifications (age, height, features, job... everything) and then you try to find that person - only to discover that your "average man" doesn't really exist anywhere. It comes from averaging out things, you'd expect to find him almost everywhere, but in reality, you'd be happy to find one in nation of millions of people.

author: @sergewind2208
text: The reason monkeys and humans are alive at the same time is that the monkeys that are currently alive are not our ancestors, they like us are offspring of our (shared) ancestors. At some point, the proto-ape species split into two, and the two halves of the one proto-ape species had different evolutionary niches to fill and eventually started diverging even more, then we had the official "speciation moment" when the two halves couldn't make viable offspring, and you can consider them two different species. There's more svience and history to it than that but that's the gist of is.

replies.author: @joshplotnik1802
replies.text: This is true but it’s also true that different populations of the same species can evolve independently when facing different environment pressures. So, one group of apes may eventually evolve into humans while a different group of the same ape, not facing environmental pressures for change, may not evolve much at all.

replies.author: @Kaireddin-BB
replies.text: That's not the reason though, it's not as if you turn a switch and change a whole species into another. Look at dogs and wolves today, they can still breed and they're still considered a single species, but eventually it might happen that dogs diverge so much they can't breed with wolves anymore, that wouldn't make wolves extinct just because a new species is formed, especially since dogs are mostly domestic and thus occupy a different ecological niche that doesn't compete with the wolves'.
